findings.claim: The diff lowers the default-on threshold for VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE from `is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.11.0.dev")` (effectively never on stable) to `is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.10.0")`. This flips AOT compile from OFF-by-default to ON-by-default for all users on the stable torch 2.10 release. When VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE is truthy, __call__ in decorators.py enters the AOT path: it computes aot_compile_hash_factors, builds a cache_dir, and attempts torch.compiler.load_compiled_function; on a cache miss (fresh install / changed source / hash mismatch) it silently swallows the exception and proceeds through the full AOT compile+serialize path. Enabling this static default broadly changes the compile/startup path for the entire torch-2.10 user base at once — the same class of default-flip that historical cases flagged for VLLM_USE_STANDALONE_COMPILE (default "0"->"1") with compile-or-startup-time symptoms. The default value itself, not routing logic, selects the new path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure cold-start / first-compilation and warm-restart (cache-hit vs cache-miss) engine startup time on torch 2.10 with VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE unset (new default on) vs explicitly =0, across a representative model, to quantify the startup-latency delta introduced by flipping the default.

findings.claim: This diff is itself a fix for a suboptimal-kernel-gate-style regression, and it removes the regression rather than introducing one. In the parent, the caller in fused_moe.py passed `naive_block_assignment` positionally into `moe_lora_align_block_size(...)`. In the GPU implementation (punica_gpu.py:325-333) the positional parameter order is `(topk_ids, num_tokens, block_size, num_experts, max_loras, adapter_enabled, expert_map=None, pad_sorted_ids=False, naive_block_assignment=False)`. The 8th positional argument therefore bound to `pad_sorted_ids`, leaving `naive_block_assignment` stuck at its default `False`. As a result the fast naive-block-assignment path (which skips the expensive `ops.moe_lora_align_block_size` alignment kernel plus the sorted_ids/expert_ids/num_tokens_post_pad tensor allocations and instead just does `expert_ids = topk_ids.reshape(-1)`) was never taken, even when the sparsity heuristic (`num_tokens*top_k*8 <= local_num_experts*max_loras`) said it should be. The base-class signature also lacked the param entirely. The fix adds `naive_block_assignment=False` to the base signature and switches the call site to a keyword argument, so the gate now correctly selects the cheap path. Net effect is a performance improvement (removal of pointless alignment-kernel + allocation work in the sparse MoE-LoRA decode case), not a new regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_lora.py moe_lora_align_block_size / fused_moe_lora with a small-batch sparse config satisfying num_tokens*top_k*8 <= local_num_experts*max_loras, comparing naive_block_assignment=True vs False

findings.claim:
This diff adds a new `enable_ep_weight_filter` config option that DEFAULTS to `False`, and inserts it as a required third condition in the guard of `DefaultModelLoader._init_ep_weight_filter`:

    if not (
        model_config.is_moe
        and parallel_config.enable_expert_parallel
        and parallel_config.enable_ep_weight_filter   # <-- new, default False
    ):
        return

At the parent commit, the EP weight filter ran unconditionally whenever `model_config.is_moe and parallel_config.enable_expert_parallel`. In that path `_init_ep_weight_filter` computes `local_expert_ids`, which is threaded into `safetensors_weights_iterator(..., local_expert_ids=self.local_expert_ids)` so each rank only reads its OWN expert shard from disk and skips non-local expert tensors.

Because the new flag defaults to False, EVERY existing user running `--enable-expert-parallel` (without also passing the brand-new `--enable-ep-weight-filter`) now takes the SLOW path: `local_expert_ids` stays `None`, the weights iterator no longer filters, and each rank re-reads the FULL set of per-expert weight tensors from disk. For large MoE checkpoints (DeepSeek, Mixtral, Kimi-K2.5) this drastically increases storage I/O and model-load time relative to the parent behavior. This is a static-default routing regression (config-toggle-perf-feature), not a runtime-predicate bug: the feature is functionally intact but is disabled by default, silently reverting the previously-automatic I/O reduction.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-rank model-load wall time and bytes read from disk for a large per-expert MoE checkpoint (e.g. Mixtral-8x7B or DeepSeek) under `--enable-expert-parallel` with ep_size>1, comparing parent (filter always on) vs this commit with the flag left at its default (off) and explicitly enabled.

findings.claim: This PR flips the static default of `disable_cascade_attn` from `False` to `True` in `ModelConfig`. Cascade attention is a pure throughput optimization (prefix-state reuse): when many requests share a long common prefix, the shared prefix's KV is read once instead of re-read per request (memory-bandwidth bound). Because `disable_cascade_attn` propagates to `EngineArgs.disable_cascade_attn = ModelConfig.disable_cascade_attn` (arg_utils.py:448) and then to `GPUModelRunner.cascade_attn_enabled = not self.model_config.disable_cascade_attn` (gpu_model_runner.py:450), changing the default to True disables the feature globally by default. Even though cascade attention was already gated by a beneficial-heuristic at runtime (the docstring: 'even when set to False, cascade attention will only be used when the heuristic tells that it's beneficial'), that heuristic never runs now because the master switch defaults off. All users with shared-prefix workloads (e.g. large system prompts, few-shot templates, multi-turn chat with common context) lose the memory-bandwidth savings unless they explicitly opt in with `--disable-cascade-attn False`. This is exactly the historical config-toggle-perf-feature mechanism: a static default value routing to the slower non-cascade path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput/TTFT on a high-concurrency workload with a long shared prefix (e.g. 2k-token common system prompt, batch of 64-256 requests) comparing disable_cascade_attn=False vs True; measure attention kernel memory bandwidth and aggregate tokens/sec.

findings.claim: The parent code called tex.thd_get_partitioned_indices() once per key inside the `for key, data in batch.items()` loop, recomputing an identical partitioned-index tensor for every batch entry. Since all entries in `batch` share the same sequence dimension (data.size(1)) and the same cu_seqlens_q_padded / cp_size / cp_rank, the computation is loop-invariant. This diff hoists the call out of the loop and computes `index` a single time using `batch[next(iter(batch))].size(1)`, then reuses it for every `data.index_select(1, index)`. This matches the retrieved 'redundant-collective' prior (LLaVA CP path eliminating repeated per-key CUDA work in the dataloader): the change is a legitimate elimination of a redundant per-key call, i.e. a performance IMPROVEMENT, not a regression. No new regression is introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: LLaVA THD-format CP dataloader micro-benchmark: measure per-step host/device time of the batch partitioning loop with a multi-key batch (input_ids, labels, position_ids, loss_mask) and CP>1, comparing per-key vs hoisted thd_get_partitioned_indices.

findings.claim: This diff is a performance IMPROVEMENT, not a regression. In _CudagraphReplayNode.forward, the parent unconditionally called `cudagraph_input.copy_(user_input)` for every non-static input on each cudagraph replay (a hot per-step path). The change guards the device-to-device copy behind `if user_input.data_ptr() != cudagraph_input.data_ptr()`, so when the user tensor already aliases the cudagraph input buffer (the common case where an upstream graph's output buffer is reused directly as the next graph's input), the redundant copy_ is skipped. This directly matches the historical 'redundant-buffer-copy' mechanism (wasted copy on a correctly-sized buffer, verified by data_ptr aliasing) but eliminates it rather than introducing it. No new copy/alloc is added; buffer sizing/layout is unchanged. The guard is safe because the earlier static-input branch already asserts data_ptr equality, confirming aliasing is an expected condition.

findings.claim: The diff flips the default of `--data-parallel-sharding-strategy` from `'no_shard'` to `'optim_grads_params'` in `_add_distributed_args`. This is a static-config default change that silently routes users (who don't explicitly set the flag, e.g. mfsdp/custom-FSDP jobs) onto the fully-sharded ZeRO-3-style path (`optim_grads_params`). In megatron_fsdp.py this default changes runtime behavior in multiple hot paths: `is_delay_grad_reduce` becomes False (no_shard/optim only), grad reduce happens every bprop, and per-parameter all-gather/reduce-scatter of params+grads+optimizer state is enabled (see megatron_fsdp.py lines 312-316, 414, 438, 648, 713, 919, 1013, 1058). Compared to `no_shard`, `optim_grads_params` inserts an all-gather of parameters before every forward/backward and a reduce-scatter of gradients, adding per-iteration collective communication and unshard/reshard overhead that is not present with the previous default. This matches the historical 'config-toggle-perf-feature' mechanism: a default value change selecting a heavier communication/latency path for all jobs that relied on the prior default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-iteration step time and communication volume (all-gather + reduce-scatter) for an mfsdp/custom-FSDP training run without an explicit --data-parallel-sharding-strategy flag, before vs after this default change; also measure small-scale DP where no_shard was previously optimal (comm-bound regime).

findings.claim: This diff is an OPTIMIZATION of the exact historical `redundant-hot-path-work` pattern, not a regression. In AsyncScheduler._update_after_schedule (hot path, per-request per-step), the parent code allocated a fresh `[-1] * self.num_spec_tokens` list object for every eligible request each scheduling step; the diff replaces this with a single reusable read-only placeholder `self._spec_token_placeholders` created once in __init__, eliminating per-step list allocation/GC churn. Similarly scheduler.py:487 replaces the in-place `del request.spec_token_ids[num_scheduled_spec_tokens:]` + reassignment with a conditional slice, and scheduler.py:889 avoids `.clear()` churn via a guarded `if request.spec_token_ids: request.spec_token_ids = []`. In MambaMixer causal_conv1d update path (mamba_mixer.py ~line 923, the per-decode-step hot path _ssm_decode), the diff adds per-call .to(conv_state.dtype) casts on xBC, the conv1d weight, and the conv1d bias, plus a trailing .to(xBC_dtype) cast on the output. When conv_state_dtype == xBC.dtype (the common/default case where states match the activation dtype), these .to() calls are no-op-intent but still incur tensor-op dispatch/copies. More importantly, weight.to(...) and bias.to(...) recompute a cast of the (constant) conv1d weight/bias on EVERY decode step rather than caching a pre-cast copy, adding redundant per-step copy kernels in the innermost inference loop. Same pattern is duplicated in the prefill path (~line 780). Consider a fast-path guard (skip casting when dtypes already match) and caching the pre-cast weight/bias.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark Mamba decode-step latency (causal_conv1d_update) with matching dtypes before vs after this change to measure overhead of the added per-step .to() casts on weight/bias/x.

findings.claim: The new CLI flags --mamba-inference-conv-states-dtype and --mamba-inference-ssm-states-dtype default to 'bf16' (see arguments.py _add_inference_args). Previously the Mamba conv/ssm inference state tensors were allocated with self.params_dtype (dynamic_context.py: mamba_conv_states/mamba_ssm_states used dtype=self.params_dtype). For a model whose params_dtype is fp32, this diff silently narrows the resident Mamba inference state tensors to bf16 by default. This is the inverse-direction manifestation of the historical 'low-precision-state' pattern: the resident state cache dtype now no longer tracks the model dtype but a hardcoded default. Memory impact is favorable when narrowing, but it changes numerical behavior of inference state by default without opt-in; if a user runs fp32 inference they get bf16 states unless they override. Confirm the default is intended to be model-dtype-tracking rather than a fixed bf16.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare Mamba dynamic-inference memory footprint and per-step numerics with default (bf16) vs fp32 states for an fp32/bf16 hybrid model.

discard_output_and_register_recompute now calls _get_share_storage(), which on first invocation triggers torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline to JIT-compile a C++ extension (share_storage_ext). load_inline invokes the system C++ compiler (nvcc/g++) and can take tens of seconds to minutes on the first call, blocking the training forward pass. This adds a large one-time stall at the first checkpoint step (and a full recompile if the build cache / ninja is unavailable or a fresh container). The extension replaces a cheap in-process untyped_storage().copy_(). Although caching via the module-global _share_storage_ext avoids recompiling per call, the compilation happens synchronously inside the training loop the first time this method runs across many transformer layers/experts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time the first training iteration that hits discard_output_and_register_recompute with a cold torch_extensions build cache vs the parent copy-based implementation; measure step-time spike.

findings.claim: This diff is the *fix* for (not an introduction of) the redundant-buffer-copy pattern seen historically in the Megatron-LM wgrad-fusion path. In grouped_linear.py:1048 the previous condition `if delay_wgrad and ctx.weight_requires_grad:` returned `[None]*num_groups` even when `accumulate_into_main_grad` was active; but for the accumulate-into-main-grad + delayed-wgrad case the grad tensors are still needed to be handed back so the deferred GEMM accumulates into main_grad. The added `and not accumulate_into_main_grad` guard makes autograd skip its own gradient accumulation buffer only when the layer does NOT own the main_grad accumulation contract. Net effect is avoiding an unnecessary autograd grad-accumulation buffer (memory/copy), consistent with the historical redundant-buffer-copy remediation. No new redundant copy is introduced.

findings.claim: The change to `AiterExperts.expects_unquantized_inputs` returns `not self.moe_config.use_mori_kernels`. When paired with MoRI EP dispatch, this now returns False so the prepare step defers no quantization: it re-enables FP8-dispatch quantization inside MoriPrepareAndFinalize.prepare() (the `if self.use_fp8_dispatch and not defer_input_quant` branch), which quantizes activations to FP8 BEFORE the all-to-all dispatch. This is directionally consistent with the historical 'collective-payload-reduction' pattern (fuse activation quant into dispatch to send fp8 bytes over the wire instead of bf16) — i.e. the diff FIXES/PRESERVES the byte-reduction on the wire rather than introducing a regression. No perf regression is introduced by this branch.

findings.claim: This diff REORDERS operations to REDUCE (not introduce) a blocking device->host sync, matching the known 'blocking-d2h-sync' pattern seen in the Qwen VL vision transformers. In the parent, `cu_seqlens` was moved to `self.device` (GPU) FIRST, then `compute_attn_mask_seqlen(cu_seqlens)` (.max().item()/.tolist()) and `(cu_seqlens[1:]-cu_seqlens[:-1]).tolist()` were called on the GPU tensor, forcing a blocking cudaMemcpy D2H + stream sync per vision forward. The diff computes `max_seqlen` and `seqlens` on the still-CPU `cu_seqlens` first, then transfers to device with non_blocking=True afterward. This is a correct fix of the historical mechanism, not a regression. The change does not introduce any new host-scalar loop or blocking sync.

findings.claim: The Gumbel sampling kernel (hot decode-time sampling path, launched every step over [num_tokens, vocab_size] logits) now promotes the entire per-block logits tensor to FP64 (`logits = logits.to(tl.float64)`). The `tl.max` reduction over BLOCK_SIZE=1024 elements, the `logits + gumbel_noise` add, and the `tl.store` into `local_max` all now execute in FP64. On most GPUs FP64 arithmetic throughput is 1/8–1/64 of FP32 (only 1/2 on A100/H100), so this widening significantly slows the vocab-wide max-reduction that dominates this kernel's cost. Prior commit history shows this path was deliberately moved to FP32 (#34854 'Use FP32 for Gumbel Noise') for performance, and this PR reverts that to FP64 for numerical reasons — trading latency for precision.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark gumbel_sample() latency at production vocab sizes (e.g. 128k–256k) and batch sizes, comparing FP32 (parent) vs FP64 (this commit) on the target GPU class (consumer vs A100/H100).

findings.claim: `local_max` intermediate buffer changed from float32 to float64 in gumbel_sample(). This doubles the memory footprint and write bandwidth of the [num_tokens, num_blocks] scratch tensor, and the subsequent host-side `local_max.argmax(dim=-1)` reduction now runs over FP64 data — extra memory traffic on the sampling hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare allocation + argmax reduction time for local_max as fp32 vs fp64.

(matched: 'torch.tensor([1.0 / scale], dtype=torch.float32, device=')

The receiver cache (P1) key was changed from feature.identifier (which is LoRA-prefixed via _get_mm_identifier when enable_tower_connector_lora=True) to feature.mm_hash (the base hash without LoRA prefix): `cache_key = feature.mm_hash or feature.identifier`. This is intentional to *enable* sharing the mm_processor_cache across LoRAs (the change removes the guard in arg_utils.py that forced mm_processor_cache_gb=0 with vision LoRA). However, this is precisely the scenario the historical 'processor cache keyed incorrectly' case warns about: when enable_tower_connector_lora=True, the vision/tower embeddings genuinely DIFFER per LoRA. Keying the receiver cache by base mm_hash (dropping the LoRA discriminator) means two different LoRAs sharing the same base image hash will collide and reuse the WRONG cached embedding — a correctness bug that also silently reuses stale computed state across requests. This is the same failure family as the prefix-state-caching leaf (keying/matching reused state on the wrong key).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-LoRA vision serving (enable_tower_connector_lora=True) with two LoRAs sharing the same image; assert distinct embeddings are returned per LoRA and measure correctness + cache hit distribution.

findings.claim: P0/P1 cache-key mirroring invariant is broken. BaseMultiModalCache documents that P0 (sender) and P1 (receiver) caches MUST be updated with the same keys 'so that their cache eviction order remains the same' and 'the keys in P0 and P1 caches are mirrored'. The sender-side (P0) MultiModalProcessorSenderCache / ShmObjectStoreSenderCache key on mm_hash values derived from _get_mm_identifier (LoRA-prefixed identifier). This diff changes only the receiver side (P1) get_and_update_features to key on feature.mm_hash (non-prefixed base hash), while the sender still keys on the LoRA-prefixed identifier. When these diverge (i.e. exactly when enable_tower_connector_lora is active and prefixes differ per LoRA), the two LRU caches diverge in membership and eviction order. P0 decides whether to set mm_input=None for IPC based on its (LoRA-prefixed) key; if P1 now stores under a different (base) key, P0 may skip sending data that P1 does not actually have cached, or hit/miss inconsistently — undermining the IPC-skip optimization and cache-eviction assumptions.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With IPC caching enabled + vision LoRA, measure IPC bytes transferred (pixel-value serialization) and P0 vs P1 cache hit/eviction parity across a stream of requests reusing images across LoRAs.

findings.claim: The new persistent q_concat_buffer is sized to (max_num_batched_tokens, num_heads, head_size) bf16 and reserved via get_simultaneous during __init__. For DeepSeek-V3.2 (num_heads=128, head_size=576) with e.g. max_num_batched_tokens=8192, that is 8192*128*576*2 bytes ≈ 1.13 GB of workspace reserved unconditionally (both in the fp8_ds_mla and non-fp8 branch). Unlike the previous torch.cat which allocated only for the actual num_tokens per step, this buffer is reserved at the worst-case size for the whole run. Verify the workspace budget: this could inflate peak memory / reduce KV-cache blocks (throughput regression under memory pressure), especially since head_size (576) here includes rope; note bench/tests use nope_dim+rope=576 output but per-head allocated width equals head_size passed at init.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory / available KV cache blocks before vs after with DeepSeek-V3.2 at max_num_batched_tokens=8192 and confirm no reduction in usable KV cache.

findings.claim: This diff is itself the positive form of the historical [kernel-fusion]/[unfused-transpose-copy] pattern: it replaces torch.cat((ql_nope, q_pe), dim=-1) (which materializes a fresh contiguous output every forward and, given ql_nope is a non-contiguous transposed BMM output, forces a strided gather + copy) with a single vectorized concat_mla_q CUDA kernel writing into a preallocated buffer. This is an optimization, not a regression, and directly matches the mechanism of the retrieved kernel-fusion case (avoid torch.cat, use preallocated buffer + copy kernel). No new redundant copy is introduced on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/bench_concat_mla_q.py (already added) across NUM_TOKENS to confirm concat_mla_q <= torch.cat latency.

This diff changes how Qwen3-VL computes the dummy/profiling video size used for memory profiling. In the parent, get_max_video_tokens() and Qwen3VLDummyInputsBuilder used num_frames = get_num_frames_with_most_features(...) which returns min(...,_MAX_FRAMES_PER_VIDEO=2048) frames sized at the image-max resolution. The new code hard-codes num_frames=2 for both token accounting (get_max_video_tokens) and dummy video generation, while replacing the per-frame spatial size with a video-specific max (video_max_pixels // temporal_patch_size). This is a profiling-accounting change: the estimate of max_num_video_tokens (used to size the memory-profiling run) now depends on 2 frames rather than 2048. If the new spatial-size compensation does not exactly reproduce the true worst-case token count, the memory-profiling accounting for videos will be off, mirroring the historical Qwen3-VL profiling-accounting issue where _MAX_FRAMES_PER_VIDEO / dummy-video sizing mis-estimated max video tokens. Verify get_max_video_tokens still returns the true maximum vision-embedding count so KV/activation memory is neither over- nor under-provisioned.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare reported max_num_video_tokens and peak profiling memory for Qwen3-VL before/after this commit against an actual 2048-frame max-resolution video to confirm accounting matches real worst case.

findings.claim: The refactor replaces `relax_for_mixed_batch_cudagraphs()` with mode-conditional `replace(...)` calls in both key registration (`initialize_cudagraph_keys`) and dispatch lookup (`dispatch`). This is exactly the cudagraph-enablement/dispatch-key mechanism the historical priors warn about: capture keys and dispatch keys must be constructed identically, or `dispatch()` silently returns `(CUDAGraphMode.NONE, ...)` and the model runs eager on hot decode/prefill path (GPU bubble / latency regression) despite graphs having been captured. Key risk area: for pure FULL mode, keys are now registered with `num_reqs` SET (previously relaxed to `num_reqs=None`), and dispatch looks up with `replace(batch_desc, uniform=False)` keeping `num_reqs`. `num_reqs` at dispatch is `min(num_tokens_padded, max_num_seqs)` (non-uniform) or `num_tokens_padded//uniform_decode_query_len` (uniform); registration for mixed uses non-uniform num_reqs. If a uniform-decode batch is dispatched in pure FULL mode, dispatch computes `num_reqs = num_tokens_padded//uniform_decode_query_len` then only overrides `uniform=False`, leaving the uniform num_reqs — which may NOT equal the registered non-uniform `min(num_tokens_padded, max_num_seqs)` when `uniform_decode_query_len>1` (spec-decode), causing a key miss and fallback to NONE.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-throughput/latency test in FULL cudagraph mode with spec-decode enabled (uniform_decode_query_len>1); assert dispatch() returns FULL (not NONE) for captured decode sizes so cudagraphs are actually replayed.

findings.claim: scheduler_metadata buffer sizing changed from `max_num_seqs + 1` to `1 + round_up(max(max_num_seqs, max_cudagraph_size), 4) * 4` in both flash_attn.py and flashattn_mla.py. This multiplies the persistent int32 buffer size roughly 4x and additionally takes the max with max_cudagraph_capture_size, which can be larger than max_num_seqs. The buffer is int32 and modest in absolute size (per-metadata-builder), so this is not a critical regression, but it is a new persistent allocation growth proportional to the larger of the two dims that did not exist before. This is a required correctness change for the new FA3 swizzle layout (4 slots per batch element), not wasted work, so severity is low.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-layer scheduler_metadata GPU memory footprint before/after with large max_cudagraph_capture_size (e.g. 512+) and many attention layers; confirm total persistent allocation delta is acceptable.

findings.claim: The probabilistic rejection-sampling path adds a persistent per-request draft-logits cache. RequestState now allocates `draft_logits` of shape [max_num_reqs, num_speculative_steps, vocab_size] in model_dtype whenever `cache_draft_logits=not use_strict_rejection_sampling` (i.e. method='probabilistic'). This is a large device tensor (max_num_reqs * num_spec_steps * vocab_size elements) that lives for the whole run, cutting into KV-cache memory. The config docstring itself acknowledges 'at the cost of more memory to cache draft logits.' This is a genuine memory-capacity cost introduced by the diff, gated behind the non-default method.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory / available KV cache blocks with rejection_sample_method='probabilistic' vs 'strict' at large max_num_reqs and vocab_size (e.g. 128k).

findings.claim: probabilistic_rejection_sample() runs two full-vocab softmax passes (`torch.softmax(target_logits)` over [num_draft_tokens+num_reqs, V] and `torch.softmax(draft_logits)` over [num_reqs, num_spec_steps, V]) plus several extra Triton kernels (probabilistic reject kernel, residual-logits kernel) on every decode step in the spec-decode hot path. These are large read/write passes over vocab-sized tensors added per step relative to the strict path which only ran one small [num_reqs] Triton kernel. Only incurred when method='probabilistic'.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-step decode latency for spec-decode with probabilistic vs strict rejection sampling, varying vocab_size and num_speculative_tokens.

findings.claim: Enabling FP8 MoE for gfx12x (adding is_rocm_on_gfx12x to device_supports_fp8 in TritonExperts) newly routes gfx12x devices into the FP8 w8a8 fused-MoE path. Only two tuned config shapes are shipped (E=128,N=512 and E=64,N=768). Any other MoE shape run on gfx12x that now enables FP8 will have NO matching tuned config file and will fall back to the generic/default Triton launch parameters — exactly the historical throughput-regression mechanism (missing per-shape config -> default/fallback tiling). This is only a regression risk for gfx12x MoE shapes outside the two tuned Qwen models; for those two models the configs cover it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a non-Qwen FP8 MoE model on gfx12x and compare throughput before/after enabling is_rocm_on_gfx12x to confirm fallback-config path is not slower than the previous (non-FP8) path

findings.claim: The PR adds two device-specific tuned fused-MoE Triton config JSONs for AMD_Radeon_R9700 (gfx1201): E=128,N=512 and E=64,N=768, fp8_w8a8, block_shape=[128,128]. This is the healthy/positive side of the historical kernel-config-tuning pattern: it PROVIDES tuned launch params (BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, GROUP_SIZE_M, num_warps, num_stages, waves_per_eu) so the grouped-GEMM kernel avoids the generic default/fallback tiling on this device. No regression from the JSONs themselves — they only take effect for the exact E/N/device_name/dtype/block_shape key. Verify the runtime device_name resolves to 'AMD_Radeon_R9700' (added to _ROCM_DEVICE_ID_NAME_MAP as 0x7551) so get_config_file_name lookup hits these files rather than falling back.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmark_moe.py on gfx1201 R9700 for Qwen3-30B-A3B-FP8 tp=2 across M buckets 1..4096, compare vs untuned fallback config

findings.claim: In `update_scheduler_for_invalid_drafts`, the code calls `num_valid_draft_tokens_event.synchronize()` and then reads per-request values from the CPU buffer via `int(num_valid_draft_tokens_cpu[req_index].item())` inside a Python `for req_id in req_data.req_ids:` loop. This forces a full GPU->CPU synchronization (waiting on the CUDA event) and then does per-element `.item()` reads in a host loop. This lands on the critical scheduling path for every step when ngram_gpu spec-decode is active, blocking the GPU pipeline (defeats the whole 'async scheduler compatible' goal). This matches the blocking-d2h-sync pattern: reading a device value to host forces a sync, and the arithmetic/looping is done on host afterward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: ngram_gpu spec-decode throughput with async scheduling enabled, measuring per-step scheduler overhead vs batch size

findings.claim: static rule 'tensor-on-gpu-before-cpu-sync' matched antipattern: Building a small metadata tensor directly on the GPU (device=self.device) that is later consumed by a CPU-synchronizing op like .item()/.tolist(), forcing a blocking device-to-host sync. (matched: 'torch.tensor(\n                padded_list, dtype=torch.int32, device=self.device')

When TMA is enabled (SM90+, not fully_sharded) and sorted_token_ids is not None, the a_intermediate_cache1 allocation shape changes from (num_slices, M, top_k_num, max_lora_rank) to (num_slices, sorted_token_ids.shape[0], EM, max_lora_rank). This is a per-forward torch.zeros allocation on the hot MoE-LoRA path. sorted_token_ids.shape[0] == max_loras (the max_loras dim), and EM == sorted_token_ids.shape[1] (the padded per-lora token slots). The product max_loras*EM can substantially exceed M*top_k_num when many loras are provisioned but few are active, or when block padding inflates EM — inflating both the zeroing cost and peak memory of the intermediate cache each call. This is exactly the config-toggle style trap where enabling a feature (TMA fast path) silently enlarges a hot-path buffer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE-LoRA forward on SM90 with max_loras=8 but 1-2 active loras; measure intermediate_cache alloc size and per-call latency of add_lora_fused_moe with/without TMA

findings.claim: The new admission gate `can_fit_full_sequence` is invoked inside the hot waiting-loop (scheduler.py:557 `while (self.waiting or self.skipped_waiting) and token_budget > 0:`) once per candidate request per scheduling step, and it re-runs the full `self.coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate(...)` fan-out over all `single_type_managers`. Immediately afterwards `allocate_slots()` runs the SAME `get_num_blocks_to_allocate` computation again (and its own free-block check). When `scheduler_reserve_full_isl` is True (the new default), every admitted request pays this coordinator fan-out twice per step — a duplicated per-request scheduler-hot-path cost. This is the same class of hot-path redundant coordinator work seen in the kernel-noop-skip case (allocate_slots fanning out through the coordinator on every request).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput/scheduler-overhead benchmark with many small waiting requests (e.g. high-QPS short-prompt sweep) comparing --scheduler-reserve-full-isl=True vs False, measuring schedule() step latency.

findings.claim: `scheduler_reserve_full_isl` defaults to True, so the extra full-ISL admission check runs for ALL deployments by default, including chunked-prefill / large-context workloads where the full sequence never fits early and requests get repeatedly rejected+re-evaluated each step (the encoder_cache_manager.free + break path), potentially wasting per-step scheduling work and reducing admitted concurrency vs prior behavior. Consider defaulting to False or short-circuiting the check when full_num_tokens already equals num_new_tokens (i.e. non-chunked case) to avoid redundant computation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Long-context chunked-prefill throughput benchmark comparing admission behavior and effective batch concurrency with the flag on vs off.

findings.claim: The new `NixlConnector.prefer_cross_layer_blocks` property parses the opt-in flag as `bool(str(extra_config.get("enable_cross_layers_blocks", "False")))`. `str(...)` of any value (including the default string "False") yields a non-empty string, and `bool(non_empty_string)` is always `True`. Thus the guard misfires: cross-layer blocks is effectively ON whenever the backend is FLASH_ATTN/FLASHINFER, even for users who left it at the default "False" or explicitly set it to "False". This silently changes the KV-cache physical layout (`allocate_uniform_kv_caches` / cross-layer contiguous allocation, `split_k_and_v` disabled, single 'ALL_LAYERS' registration) for all such deployments, rather than the intended opt-in. Compare to the docs which state 'By default, this feature is disabled.' The correct parse should be a real truthiness check (e.g. `str(...).lower() == "true"`). Note also inconsistency: the accuracy-test shell script writes the key `cross_layers_blocks` while the code reads `enable_cross_layers_blocks`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: P/D NIXL transfer with FLASH_ATTN backend and no cross-layer config set: compare KV cache registration path (num registered regions, tensor layout) and end-to-end transfer latency vs parent to confirm the layout is not silently forced ON.

findings.claim: Because `prefer_cross_layer_blocks` returns True for the default config on FLASH_ATTN/FLASHINFER, the compatibility hash factor `cross_layers_blocks` (added to compute_nixl_compatibility_hash) also flips to True unexpectedly. A prefill/decode pair where one side leaves the flag default and the other side runs an older build (or a backend that returns False, e.g. TRITON_ATTN) will produce mismatched compat hashes and fail the handshake compatibility check, forcing repeated failed handshakes / aborted transfers — a P/D interoperability and wasted-handshake-work regression driven by the same misparsed predicate.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Heterogeneous P/D: prefill on FLASH_ATTN (default flag) vs decode on TRITON_ATTN; measure handshake success rate and retry/abort counts vs parent.

findings.claim: The cache key changes from graph_index (int) to graph_hash (sha256 of a pickled/sanitized GraphModule). This is a deliberate cold-start optimization: previously each of the N piecewise subgraphs got a distinct index-based key, so identical subgraphs (e.g. the 3 unique transformer-layer subgraphs repeated across 32 layers) were re-driven through compile/AOTAutograd (index-keyed => 3 misses + 30 hits per the old test). Content-hash keying lets identical subgraphs share one compiled artifact and skip work entirely (new test: 3 misses, 0 further compilation). This aligns with the compile-cache-misconfig family (key defeats/enables reuse) but here it *improves* reuse rather than regressing it. The correctness risk to watch is that graph_hash must fully capture everything that distinguishes two subgraphs' compiled outputs; the hash is computed via AOTAutogradCachePickler(graph)+sanitize_gm_for_cache, which is the same content basis torch uses for its own cache, so collision risk is low. No regression introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: test_cold_start.py cold-start compilation counters (autograd_cache_miss/hit) + wall-clock cold start time for a multi-layer model

findings.claim: static rule 'len-of-list-building-getter' matched antipattern: Calling len() on the result of an accessor that materializes/copies a full collection (e.g. get_token_ids(), get_tokens(), to_list()) just to obtain a length, incurring an O(L) allocation instead of an O(1) length lookup. (matched: 'len(blocks.get_block_ids())')

The rewritten `HybridKVCacheCoordinator.find_longest_cache_hit` replaces a fixed 2-pass (full-attn then other-attn) algorithm with an outer `while True` fixed-point loop that, on every convergence miss, re-scans ALL attention groups and re-invokes each group's `find_longest_cache_hit`. This is a hot path (executed for every incoming request in `get_computed_blocks`). Whereas the parent version called each single-type `find_longest_cache_hit` exactly once, the new version can call `manager_cls.find_longest_cache_hit` for non-full-attention groups multiple times (once per outer iteration until the length stops shrinking). For hybrid models with several sliding-window/mamba groups whose accepted hit lengths differ, each pass can reduce `curr_hit_length`, triggering another full re-scan; worst case O(#groups) outer iterations each doing O(#groups) `find_longest_cache_hit` scans over the block-hash list, i.e. up to O(G^2 * B) block-pool lookups vs the old O(B) two-pass. Only full attention gets the cheap 'reuse cached blocks / truncate' fast path; sliding-window and mamba groups are re-scanned from scratch each iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark HybridKVCacheCoordinator.find_longest_cache_hit on a 3-4 group hybrid config (e.g. full+sliding_window+sliding_window_large+mamba) with a long block_hash prefix (hundreds of blocks) and partial hits at different lengths per group; compare wall time / number of manager.find_longest_cache_hit invocations vs the 2-group parent path.

findings.claim: In capture_model(), the per-iteration capture loop now inserts an extra `torch.accelerator.synchronize()` after each `_capture_cudagraphs(...)` call, in addition to the existing `torch.accelerator.synchronize()` immediately after the loop. Similarly `_capture_cudagraphs` itself now calls `torch.accelerator.synchronize()` after each `self._warmup_and_capture(...)` (i.e. per batch descriptor). Since the next capture step (or the post-loop mem_get_info) already establishes ordering, these per-step device synchronizes are defensive: they serialize the host with the device on every captured shape during startup and add measurable startup/capture wall time on many-shape configs, without being required for correctness of the subsequent mem_get_info reads. This matches the 'defensive-oversync' historical pattern (unnecessary syncs added out of caution around memory-profiling / capture, where the following read establishes its own ordering).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine startup / capture_model() wall time with a large cudagraph_capture_sizes list (e.g. many decode shapes), comparing with and without the per-descriptor/per-loop torch.accelerator.synchronize() calls.

findings.claim: profile_cudagraph_memory() calls torch.accelerator.synchronize() before/after each individual dummy-run capture sample (mem_before/free_after readback). The synchronize before mem_get_info here is genuinely required (host reads back free memory), so this leans toward legitimate; but note the whole feature runs an extra full warmup+capture pass over up to 2 descriptors per mode during startup even when VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS=0 (only the *application* of the estimate is gated by the env var, but profile_cudagraph_memory() is still invoked whenever not enforce_eager). This is extra startup compilation/capture work whose result is discarded when the env is off — a wasted-compilation-work risk during startup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare engine startup time with VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS unset (default 0) before vs after this commit; verify profile_cudagraph_memory() is not adding a redundant warmup/capture pass when its result is not applied.

findings.claim: In ipg_remove_grads, before swapping the ping-pong ipg_buffer index (bucket.index = 1 - bucket.index), this diff inserts a bidirectional cross-stream barrier: `self.reduction_stream.wait_stream(current_stream())` followed by `current_stream().wait_stream(self.reduction_stream)`. This forces the compute (current) stream and the reduction stream to fully rendezvous at every bucket swap, which is exactly the intended purpose of the fix (issue #7188 / #7371: gradient corruption where the reused ipg_buffer was overwritten before the async reduce_scatter on reduction_stream had consumed it, producing loss->0 at step 3). The barrier is therefore a correctness-required serialization, not a removable/defensive over-sync — it prevents the double-buffer from being reused while an in-flight reduction still references it.

findings.claim: In CUDAGraphWrapper.__call__, get_offloader().sync_prev_onload() is now called unconditionally on the cudagraph REPLAY hot path (right before entry.cudagraph.replay()), and get_offloader().sync_prev_onload()/join_after_forward() are added around capture. sync_prev_onload likely performs a CUDA stream synchronize (blocking the default stream on the copy_stream). Placing a stream sync on every replay serializes prefetch with replay and can add per-step latency even when offloading is disabled unless get_offloader() returns a cheap no-op offloader. Verify the no-offload path returns a null offloader whose sync_prev_onload() is a true no-op (no CUDA API call).

findings.claim: The offloader introduces stream-based async H2D prefetch (copy_stream forks/joins) with per-layer wait_prefetch/start_prefetch custom ops inserted into the model forward. This is fundamentally a transfer-bubble mechanism (like the historical 'cpu_offload.py dedicated offload_stream with per-tensor CUDA events' case): when offload_prefetch_step is too small or the CPU-GPU interconnect (PCIe) is slow, the H2D copy of offloaded expert weights (w13_weight/w2_weight for DeepSeek-V2-Lite MoE) cannot be fully hidden behind compute, producing GPU-idle bubbles per offloaded layer each forward pass. The default offload_prefetch_step=1 only prefetches one layer ahead, which may be insufficient to hide MoE weight transfer latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-forward latency / tokens-per-sec on DeepSeek-V2-Lite with --offload-prefetch-step 1 vs 2 vs 3 vs no-offload baseline to quantify unhidden transfer bubbles.

findings.claim: static rule 'split-squeeze-contiguous-chunk' matched antipattern: Splitting a fused tensor via reshape+torch.split+squeeze+.contiguous() forces redundant memory copies where a single chunk/view would suffice. (matched: '.squeeze(0).contiguous()')

The FlashInfer GDN prefill path `fi_chunk_gated_delta_rule` performs several extra full-tensor eager ops on every prefill invocation before entering the kernel: `l2norm_fwd(q)`/`l2norm_fwd(k)`, `q/k/v/g/beta.squeeze(0).contiguous()` (forcing materialized copies), and dtype up-casts `initial_state.to(float32)`, `g.to(float32)`, `beta.to(float32)` plus `torch.exp(fi_g)`. Because this is wrapped in a CustomOp whose `forward` dispatches through `self._forward_method(*args, **kwargs)` (opaque to torch.compile / not fused), these preprocessing ops run as individual eager dispatches and extra device copies rather than being fused into the compiled graph. On CUDA cc90 this is now the default path for every non-spec chunk_gated_delta_rule call in the model forward. The `contiguous()` copies and the fp32 up-casts of g/beta/initial_state add per-call memory traffic and dispatch overhead versus the previous direct `chunk_gated_delta_rule(...)` call that let the FLA kernel handle l2norm/dtype internally (use_qk_l2norm_in_kernel).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Qwen3-Next prefill latency (long prompt) on H100/cc90 comparing FlashInfer path vs FLA chunk_gated_delta_rule, isolating time in fi_chunk_gated_delta_rule preprocessing (l2norm + squeeze/contiguous + fp32 casts + exp).

findings.claim: The new wvSplitKQ wrapper calls rocm_enforce_contiguous_skinny_gemm_inputs(a, b) which runs a.contiguous()/b.contiguous() unconditionally. Its hot caller rocm_per_tensor_float_w8a8_scaled_mm_impl (vllm/model_executor/layers/quantization/kernels/scaled_mm/rocm.py) passes B.t() as the first argument (a). B is the layer weight and B.t() is a transpose view whose is_contiguous() is False, so a.contiguous() now materializes a full transposed clone of the weight on EVERY forward. This path fires when A.shape[0]==1 (the token=1 decode regime), i.e. a per-token hot path, adding a wasted [N,K] device-memory copy each call. The kernel previously consumed the transposed strided view directly. Matches the historical unfused-transpose-copy mechanism (transpose + .contiguous() producing a wasted copy kernel). Note the code comment admits the tensors 'may actually be contiguous in memory' but the kernels reject is_contiguous()==False, so the copy is layout-driven rather than truly necessary.
findings.suggested_benchmark: ROCm FP8 w8a8 per-tensor decode (batch=1) linear layer latency: rocm_per_tensor_float_w8a8_scaled_mm_impl with wvSplitKQ, compare against parent to measure extra weight-transpose contiguous copy per step.

findings.claim: In create_cudagraphs(), the diff removes the transformer-layer-specific buffer-reuse optimization (optimize_transformer_layer_graph_buffers) that alternated/shared fwd hidden_state output buffers and bwd input-grad buffers across layers (clone_inputs=False path), and replaces it with unconditionally calling runner.create_fwd_graph(args, kwargs, out, clone_inputs=True). Cloning inputs on every fwd graph means each layer's cudagraph gets its own distinct input/output buffers rather than aliasing the previous layer's output, which increases per-layer buffer allocations during capture. The new TensorReusePool/make_weak_ref path is meant to compensate, but if TE weak-ref support is unavailable (HAVE_TE_GRAPHS False), the warning explicitly notes cudagraph memory overhead 'may significantly increase' -- a memory-footprint regression risk on the capture path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Capture N transformer-layer cudagraphs with and without TE weak-ref support; measure peak reserved/allocated CUDA memory after create_cudagraphs().

findings.claim: CheckpointWithoutOutput.checkpoint now short-circuits to run_function(*args) when is_graph_warmup() is True, and _recompute/discard hooks become no-ops during graph warmup. This is intentional for cudagraph correctness (recompute may be triggered in a separate warmup graph). During actual cudagraph warmup this disables activation recomputation memory savings, which is expected and bounded to warmup, but confirm is_graph_warmup() is never left True on the steady-state training path (the _set_warmup_end() shown in diff does not reset the global to False -- it lacks 'global _IS_GRAPH_WARMUP; _IS_GRAPH_WARMUP = False').
findings.suggested_benchmark: Assert is_graph_warmup() returns False after create_cudagraphs() completes; run a training step and verify activation checkpointing still reduces memory in steady state.

findings.claim: The new persistent buffer `persistent_kv_indices` is allocated as `torch.empty(self.max_model_len, max_num_kv_block, ...)` where `max_num_kv_block = (max_model_len + kv_block_size - 1) // kv_block_size`. The first dim is `max_model_len` (rows), the second scales as `max_model_len/kv_block_size`. For a large context (e.g. 128k+) with a small kv_block_size (block_size when supports_small_blocks, e.g. 16), this yields on the order of max_model_len^2/kv_block_size int32 elements — an O(max_model_len^2) allocation that can be gigabytes and dwarfs the actual `kv_indices` shape it holds (which is [num_reqs, num_blocks]). The persistent buffer is sized by max_model_len rather than by max_num_seqs/num_blocks, so it is over-scoped and can OOM / permanently reserve huge GPU memory. This matches the historical 'buffer-sizing-layout' pattern where a dense tensor sized by (max_reqs, vocab_size)/large dims was allocated far larger than needed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate FlexAttentionMetadataBuilder with a large max_model_len (e.g. 131072) and small kv_block_size and measure peak GPU memory from persistent_kv_indices allocation vs. actual kv_indices usage.

findings.claim: The new O2 optimization level is applied as the *default* (optimization_level=O2) and enables noop + fusion passes by default (enable_noop=True, enable_fusion=enable_fusion(cfg)), whereas at parent these PassConfig fields defaulted to False. This changes the default compilation path: more inductor passes are now run by default. This is intended as a perf improvement (fusion), but it increases startup/compilation time and, more importantly, changes the default runtime graph for ALL users who don't specify anything. Any regression in the fusion passes now hits everyone by default. The docs explicitly state 'The primary difference is that noop & fusion flags are enabled.' Because is_custom_op_enabled('rms_norm'/'quant_fp8') decides enable_fusion, fusion may silently turn off/on depending on custom_ops resolution order in __post_init__, which was reworked in this diff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare default (no flags) startup/compile time and steady-state throughput at parent vs this commit for a dense FP8 model (e.g. RedHatAI/Llama-3.2-1B-FP8) and a non-quant model; verify fusion passes actually engage and that graph capture count is unchanged.

findings.claim: enable_fusion is computed via enable_fusion(cfg) which returns True only if is_custom_op_enabled('rms_norm') or is_custom_op_enabled('quant_fp8'). is_custom_op_enabled asserts 'none' in custom_ops otherwise, and reads the +/-op tokens. The custom_ops list is only populated with 'none'/'all' earlier in __post_init__, and the '+quant_fp8'/'+rms_norm' entries are appended at various points (blocked-weights branch, sequence-parallelism branch) whose ordering relative to _apply_optimization_level_defaults matters. If _apply_optimization_level_defaults runs before '+rms_norm' is appended (SP branch runs AFTER defaults are applied in the diff), enable_fusion may be evaluated against a custom_ops list that does not yet include rms_norm/quant_fp8, causing fusion to be silently disabled in cases where it should be on -> missed fusion, throughput/latency regression on the default path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate VllmConfig for a model with blocked FP8 weights and default -O2; assert compilation_config.pass_config.enable_fusion is True and that the quant_fp8/rms_norm fusion pass runs. Compare fusion-enabled graph vs parent.

findings.claim: TEActivationOp.__new__ activation-op selection is buggy in a way that undermines the intended fusion benefit and can silently pick the wrong fused kernel. In the gated branch the third condition duplicates `config.activation_func == F.silu` (should be F.relu) so ReGLU is never selected (dead branch). In the non-gated branch F.relu is never handled and `config.activation_func == F.silu` is mapped to te.pytorch.ops.ReLU (wrong op). Since config validation permits F.relu (activation_func in (F.gelu, F.silu, F.relu)), a valid F.relu config falls through to layer_type=None and raises at build time, while non-gated F.silu silently runs ReLU instead of the intended fused activation — both defeat the fused-activation speedup this PR advertises (and the latter is also a correctness issue).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test each (activation_func, gated_linear_unit) combo maps to the expected te.pytorch.ops op; throughput/correctness for relu and non-gated silu configs

findings.claim: New opt-in path use_te_activation_func replaces MCore's fused activation kernels (bias_geglu_impl/bias_swiglu_impl/weighted_bias_swiglu_impl) with TransformerEngine fused activation operators. No default-path throughput regression is introduced; this matches the kernel-fusion prior only as an opt-in alternative, not a regression by default.

findings.claim: In FlashMLAMetadataBuilder._build_decode (flashmla.py), the diff removes the entire full-CUDA-graph persistent-buffer copy scheme. Previously, when compilation_config.cudagraph_mode.has_full_cudagraphs() was true, the freshly-allocated (per-step, varying-size) tile_scheduler_metadata and num_splits tensors from get_mla_metadata were copied into pre-allocated static buffers (cg_buf_tile_scheduler_metadata / cg_buf_num_splits) and the trailing num_splits entries were filled — so the tensors handed to the captured decode kernel had stable device addresses. After the change, for the non-fp8 (BF16 KV cache) decode path, scheduler_metadata is returned directly from get_mla_metadata with no copy into the persistent buffers. Under full cudagraph mode the captured graph will keep reading the address captured at capture time while the builder now produces new tensors each step, causing either a hard cudagraph replay error / fallback or silently stale scheduler metadata. The cg_buf_* buffers are still allocated in __init__ but are now dead. This matches the historical FlashMLA full-cudagraph static-buffer regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepSeek MLA decode with VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=FLASHMLA under full cudagraph mode (cudagraph_mode with has_full_cudagraphs) on Hopper, BF16 KV cache; verify capture/replay succeeds and decode throughput/accuracy matches eager.

findings.claim: In FlashMLASparseMetadataBuilder (flashmla_sparse.py), both _build_fp8_mixed_decode_prefill and _build_fp8_separate_prefill_decode remove the copies into the persistent full-CG buffers (tile_scheduler_metadata_buffer / num_splits_buffer) and instead store the raw scheduler_metadata returned by get_mla_metadata directly into FP8KernelMetadata. The builder declares _cudagraph_support = AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH, so these metadata tensors are consumed by captured graphs; feeding non-persistent, per-step-allocated scheduler metadata into a captured graph reintroduces the same address-stability hazard the removed buffer-copy code was written to avoid. The persistent buffers (self.tile_scheduler_metadata_buffer / self.num_splits_buffer) are now allocated but unused.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepSeek-V3.2 sparse MLA FP8 decode under UNIFORM_BATCH full cudagraph on Hopper/Blackwell; verify capture+replay stability across varying batch sizes and that decode does not fall back to eager.

findings.claim: MistralForCausalLM previously mapped to ("llama", "LlamaForCausalLM") in registry.py, so Mistral models reused the exact same compiled forward as Llama (LlamaModel.forward). After this diff, Mistral now uses a brand-new MistralModel class (a subclass of LlamaModel) with its own @support_torch_compile decorator. Because torch.compile keys/artifacts are derived from the class/forward identity (see _model_hash_key using fn.__qualname__ + co_firstlineno, and the per-class __bases__ manipulation in _support_torch_compile), MistralModel is a distinct compilation unit. This means Mistral now triggers a full separate torch.compile pass and (with AOT) a separate on-disk artifact directory, rather than reusing Llama's already-compiled graph. Since MistralModel.forward is structurally identical to LlamaModel.forward (it isn't even overridden), this is duplicated/wasted compilation work at startup for a graph that is effectively the same as Llama's.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure cold-start torch.compile / AOT-compile time and cache-artifact reuse when loading a Mistral model before vs. after this commit (e.g. mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct), and check whether Llama+Mistral in the same session share a compiled artifact.

findings.claim: In `process_json_file`, the new early-exit (`if find_optimal_num_workers and i > max_documents: break`) only stops the CONSUMER loop that reads results from `pool.imap`. It does NOT terminate the pool. The subsequent `pool.close(); pool.join()` waits for all worker processes to finish, and `pool.imap(encoder.encode, fin, 32)` keeps pulling lines from `fin` (the entire input file) into the workers. So for the `--find-optimal-num-workers` search, each trial still tokenizes the whole input file in the background instead of only `max_documents` docs — the intended 'few short runs' become several near-full-length runs, defeating the purpose of the feature and multiplying total preprocessing cost by (number of workers-to-check). The docs claim it 'launches a few short data preprocessing runs' but the workers do not stop early. Should use `pool.terminate()` after the break (and not join), or bound the input iterator to `max_documents` lines before handing to `imap`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run `--find-optimal-num-workers --max-documents 1000 --workers-to-check 2 4 8` on a large (>>1000-line) jsonl and measure wall-clock; compare against the intended cost of processing only 1000 docs per trial. Expect the observed time to scale with full-file size, not max_documents.

findings.claim: The main loop now wraps ALL setup (nltk.download, partition-file creation, and — for partitions>1 — sentence splitting and the count of total_sample_count via full-file line iteration) inside `for num_workers in workers:`. This repeats the partition creation and the potentially expensive sequential line-count / sentence-split passes once per candidate worker count during optimal-worker search, even though these are worker-count-independent and only need to run once. Hoist them out of the worker loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With partitions>1 and keep-sequential-samples, time `--find-optimal-num-workers --workers-to-check 2 4 8` and confirm the partition/line-count phase runs 3x.

findings.claim: The new calibration dataloader tokenizes every calibration sample with padding="max_length", max_length=max_sequence_length (default 512). Previously (_hf_dataset_forword_loop_func) each calibration prompt was tokenized with tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt") and NO padding, so each of the calib_size (default 512) forward passes ran on the sample's natural (typically much shorter) token length. Now every sample is force-padded to the full 512 tokens before running simple_generate, inflating the per-sample forward compute across the entire calibration loop. In simple_generate's _forward_step_func the attention mask is built as (batch, 1, seq_len, seq_len) via torch.triu(ones(seq_len,seq_len)), so cost scales O(seq_len^2) with the padded length — matching the LLaVA 'pad every sample to fixed seq_len / always build full decoder_seq_length' redundant-hot-path prior. Calibration throughput (512 forward passes) can regress substantially when real prompts are shorter than 512 tokens.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run quantize.py PTQ calibration (--calib-size 512) on a dataset with short (<128 token) samples and compare wall-clock of the mtq.quantize calibration loop with padding="max_length" (new) vs padding="longest"/no-pad (old) to measure the added forward cost from full-length padding.

findings.claim: The migration to RerunStateMachineConfig preserves rerun_mode default = 'validate_results' (matching the parent's argparse default at arguments.py:2411). This is NOT a newly introduced regression by this diff — the perf-relevant default was already 'validate_results' before the change. The historical [config-toggle-perf-feature] case describes flipping the default from 'disabled' to 'validate_results' (enabling per-iteration all_reduce in should_run_forward_backward), but that flip does not occur here; the value is unchanged. No new hot-path toggle regression is introduced.

The '--check-for-spiky-loss' flag was previously an opt-in store_true (default False, arguments.py:2177). In the new dataclass it is preserved as check_for_spiky_loss: bool = False, and the ArgumentGroupFactory maps a bool default==False to action='store_true' (argument_utils.py). So the default remains disabled — no toggle flip that would enable an extra per-iteration loss check by default. Behavior is preserved.

findings.claim: static rule 'async-busy-poll-sleep-zero' matched antipattern: An async loop that unconditionally does work every iteration and only yields via asyncio.sleep(0), busy-polling the event loop and burning CPU instead of waiting on an event/condition. (matched: 'await asyncio.sleep(0)')

static rule 'tensor-from-pylist-on-device' matched antipattern: Constructing a tensor from a Python list literal with a non-CPU device forces a CPU materialization plus a host-to-device copy on every call; use torch.zeros/ones/full/empty directly on the device instead. (matched: 'torch.tensor([token_ids], device=')

findings.claim: This diff extends get_kv_cache_quant_algo_string to also read the new modelopt 'kv_cache_scheme' key (dict form: {dynamic:false, num_bits:8, type:float}) and resolve it to "fp8" -> "fp8_e4m3". This function feeds resolve_kv_cache_dtype_string(), which is invoked at engine startup (arg_utils.py:1394) when kv_cache_dtype=='auto'. The consequence is that for modelopt-quantized models that previously did NOT match (because they exported kv_cache_scheme rather than kv_cache_quant_algo), the KV cache dtype now auto-resolves to fp8_e4m3 instead of the model's compute dtype. This matches the historical 'low-precision-state' pattern (reverted #29980 / the resolve_kv_cache_dtype_string case): it silently narrows resident KV cache tensors to 8-bit, halving KV cache memory but also altering numerical precision/accuracy without an explicit user opt-in. This is a behavior/precision-state change gated by the model's own config, not a hot-loop regression; it broadens the set of configs that trigger the auto fp8 KV cache.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a modelopt model whose hf quantization_config exports kv_cache_scheme={dynamic:false,num_bits:8,type:float} with kv_cache_dtype left at 'auto'; assert resolved cache dtype and compare KV cache memory footprint and output accuracy vs. compute-dtype KV cache.
